enrichment.short_description: Conversion Tools (conversiontools.io) is a browser-based file conversion service that converts documents, spreadsheets, images, audio, video, and data files such as JSON, XML, Excel, PDF, and Word between more than 100 formats. It targets individuals and businesses that need quick, no-install file conversion plus an API for developers who want to embed conversion into their own workflows. The service also offers a large-file mode for uploads up to 100 GB.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription plus a separate pay-as-you-go API pricing tier
enrichment.starting_price: Free tier available; paid plans start around 7 USD per month for 100 MB files and 200 monthly conversions
enrichment.headquarters: Not publicly disclosed (domain contact information lists an address in Reykjavik, Iceland, but the operating company is not publicly named)
enrichment.seo_title: Conversion Tools Review: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Conversion Tools (conversiontools.io) does, how its file converter pricing works, and whether its 100+ format support fits your workflow.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Conversion Tools, hosted at conversiontools.io, is an online file conversion utility that lets users convert documents, spreadsheets, presentations, images, audio, video, and structured data files (including JSON, XML, Excel, CSV, PDF, and Word) between more than 100 file formats directly in a web browser, without installing desktop software. The platform is positioned as a general-purpose converter for both casual users who need a one-off conversion and businesses that need to process files programmatically through a dedicated file conversion API. Core capabilities include drag-and-drop uploads, batch conversion of multiple files at once, and support for large files up to roughly 100 GB on higher-tier plans, which distinguishes it from many free online converters that cap uploads at a few hundred megabytes. The service operates on a freemium model: a free tier allows a limited number of conversions and smaller file sizes, while paid subscription plans unlock larger file size limits, higher monthly conversion quotas, priority processing, and API access. Publicly referenced pricing places the entry paid plan at roughly 7 USD per month for around 200 conversions and 100 MB files, scaling up through mid-tier plans near 15 to 16 USD per month for 500 MB files and 500 conversions, and higher tiers around 52 USD per month for 2 GB files and 1,000 conversions; the company announced a pricing update effective April 2026 that increased most plan prices by roughly 10 to 20 percent for new subscribers, while existing subscriptions kept their prior rate. A separate API pricing page offers usage-based and subscription options for developers who want to integrate file conversion directly into their own applications rather than using the web interface. The company behind Conversion Tools does not publicly disclose its founding year, headquarters, leadership team, or employee count on its marketing site; publicly available domain registration data associates the site with an address in Reykjavik, Iceland, but this should not be treated as a confirmed corporate headquarters since ownership records are private. The site's terms of use refer generically to the 'ConversionTools Authors' as the developers and operators of the service, without naming individuals or a parent company. Because of this limited corporate transparency, Conversion Tools is best understood as a niche SaaS utility rather than a venture-backed company with a public profile. For a directory or comparison context, Conversion Tools competes with other browser-based converters such as Convertio, CloudConvert, and Online-Convert, differentiating primarily through its broad format coverage, API availability, and large-file support on paid tiers. Users considering the tool for sensitive documents should review its data handling and retention policies directly on the site, since the company's limited public disclosure makes third-party verification of its security practices difficult. Overall, Conversion Tools is a practical, narrowly scoped file conversion utility suited to ad hoc conversions and light API-driven automation, rather than a full document management or enterprise workflow platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Conversion Tools Does
paragraphs: Conversion Tools is a web-based file converter that handles a wide range of document, image, audio, video, and data formats, including JSON, XML, Excel, PDF, and Word. Users upload a file through the browser, choose an output format, and download the converted result without installing any software.
The platform also exposes an API so developers can trigger conversions programmatically from their own applications, which extends its use beyond one-off manual conversions into automated pipelines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and File Size Limits
paragraphs: Conversion Tools uses a freemium pricing structure. The free tier supports smaller files and a limited number of monthly conversions, which suits occasional users.

Paid plans raise the file size ceiling and monthly conversion quota, with published tiers ranging from roughly 7 USD per month for 100 MB files and 200 conversions up to about 52 USD per month for 2 GB files and 1,000 conversions. A pricing update took effect in April 2026, raising list prices for new subscribers while grandfathering existing subscriptions at their original rate.

long_form_blocks.heading: Company Transparency Caveats
paragraphs: Unlike many SaaS vendors, Conversion Tools does not publish a founding date, headquarters address, or team information on its site. The site's legal pages refer only to the 'ConversionTools Authors' as the operating entity.
Prospective users who need vendor accountability for compliance reasons should factor in this limited public disclosure before uploading sensitive or regulated documents.

features.description: Higher-tier plans support file uploads up to roughly 100 GB, well above typical free online converters.

features.description: A dedicated file conversion API lets developers integrate conversion functionality into their own applications, billed separately from the web app plans.

features.description: The company offers custom converter development services for specialized or proprietary format needs.

features.description: A no-cost plan allows a limited number of conversions per month for casual or occasional use.

Free tier available for occasional use without signup friction
Large file handling on paid tiers exceeds many competing converters
Developer API allows automation beyond the web interface

Pricing increased in April 2026, raising costs for new subscribers

faqs.question: What is Conversion Tools?
faqs.answer: Conversion Tools is an online file conversion service at conversiontools.io that converts documents, images, audio, video, and data files such as JSON, XML, Excel, PDF, and Word between more than 100 formats.

faqs.question: Is Conversion Tools free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free tier is available with limited file sizes and monthly conversion counts. Paid plans remove some of these limits and add API access.

faqs.question: Does Conversion Tools offer an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, a separate file conversion API is available for developers who want to trigger conversions programmatically, priced separately from the web app subscription plans.

faqs.question: How large a file can Conversion Tools handle?
faqs.answer: Higher-tier paid plans support uploads up to roughly 100 GB, though entry-level plans have much lower limits such as 100 MB.

faqs.question: Who owns Conversion Tools?
faqs.answer: The operating company is not publicly named on the site; legal pages refer only to the 'ConversionTools Authors' as the developers and operators of the service.

faqs.question: Did Conversion Tools pricing change recently?
faqs.answer: Yes, the company announced a pricing update effective April 1, 2026, that increased most plan prices for new subscribers by roughly 10 to 20 percent, while existing subscriptions kept their prior rate.

faqs.question: What formats does Conversion Tools support?
faqs.answer: It supports document formats like PDF and Word, spreadsheet formats like Excel and CSV, data formats like JSON and XML, plus common image, audio, and video formats.

faqs.question: Is Conversion Tools suitable for businesses?
faqs.answer: It can be used by businesses for occasional conversions or, via its API, for automated conversion workflows, though its limited corporate transparency may be a consideration for compliance-sensitive use.

use_cases.title: Bulk file batch processing
use_cases.description: Teams converting many files at once, such as a batch of spreadsheets or images, using a paid plan with higher conversion quotas.

enrichment.short_description: conveyor.cloud is the home of Conveyor, a Visual Studio extension and standalone Windows tool made by Keyoti Inc. that gives developers local network and remote internet access to their IIS Express or ASP.NET development server through tunnelling, without changing project configuration. It is used mainly for cross-device testing, sharing a local build with collaborators, and receiving webhooks during development.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium with a paid per-developer annual license for Pro features
enrichment.starting_price: Free tier available; Pro license starts around 49 USD per developer per year
enrichment.company_size: Small (Keyoti Inc. is a small, privately held software company)
enrichment.seo_title: conveyor.cloud (Conveyor by Keyoti) Review, Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What is Conveyor by Keyoti at conveyor.cloud? See how this Visual Studio tunnelling tool exposes IIS Express to devices and the internet, plus pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: conveyor.cloud is the product site for Conveyor, a developer tool built by Keyoti Inc., a small Canadian software company founded in 2002 and based in Comox, British Columbia. Conveyor is distributed both as a Visual Studio extension and as a standalone Windows system-tray application, and its core purpose is to expose a local IIS Express or ASP.NET development server to other devices on the same network and, through tunnelling, to the broader internet, without requiring the developer to publish the project or change IIS Express configuration files. This solves a common web development problem: testing how a site or app behaves on a phone, tablet, or a colleague's machine when the site is still running only on the developer's own laptop. Once installed, Conveyor automatically generates a shareable URL that tunnels directly to the running ASP.NET application, which developers use for responsive design checks, cross-device QA, ad hoc demos, and receiving webhook callbacks from third-party services such as Twilio during local development, since webhook providers require a publicly reachable URL that a purely local server cannot provide. The standalone tool sits in the Windows system tray, can be configured to start automatically with Windows, and in its Pro tier supports custom port mappings and other advanced configuration not available in the free version. Conveyor offers a freemium pricing model: a free tier provides basic local network and limited internet tunnelling functionality, while a paid Pro upgrade unlocks features like custom subdomains and no connection time limits. Public pricing references place the Pro license at roughly 49 USD, licensed per developer per year, consistent with Keyoti's broader practice of selling per-developer annual licenses for its other components, such as its spell-checking and search engine libraries for .NET and Java. Because Conveyor is scoped specifically to Visual Studio and IIS Express or ASP.NET workflows, it functions as a narrower, more specialized alternative to general-purpose tunnelling tools such as ngrok or Cloudflare Tunnel; its main differentiation is tight, low-friction integration directly inside the Visual Studio IDE rather than a separate command-line tool. Keyoti Inc. itself is a long-running but small, privately held company that has focused since 2002 on developer productivity components rather than broad platform products, and it does not publicly disclose detailed revenue or headcount figures. Support for Conveyor is provided via email at support@conveyor.cloud and live chat on the product site. For a software directory entry, conveyor.cloud should be described precisely as Keyoti's Conveyor tunnelling and remote-testing tool for Visual Studio and IIS Express developers, distinct from unrelated same-named products such as Conveyor CI (a cloud-native CI/CD orchestration engine), ConveyorData (a data pipeline platform), and Conveyor.com (an AI-powered customer security review platform), none of which are affiliated with Keyoti's conveyor.cloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Conveyor (conveyor.cloud) Does
paragraphs: Conveyor is a Visual Studio extension and standalone Windows utility from Keyoti Inc. that tunnels a developer's local IIS Express or ASP.NET application to other devices on the local network and, optionally, to the public internet.

It removes the need to publish a project or edit IIS Express configuration files just to preview a site on a phone, tablet, or a teammate's machine, and it generates a shareable URL usable for webhook testing with services like Twilio.

long_form_blocks.heading: Free vs. Pro Pricing
paragraphs: Conveyor's free tier covers basic local network access and limited internet tunnelling, sufficient for casual cross-device checks.

The Pro tier, licensed per developer per year at roughly 49 USD based on published pricing, adds custom subdomains, removes connection time limits, and enables custom port mappings for more advanced testing setups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Not to Be Confused With Other 'Conveyor' Products
paragraphs: Several unrelated products share the Conveyor name, including Conveyor CI (a cloud-native CI/CD orchestration engine), ConveyorData (a data pipeline build and deploy platform), and Conveyor.com (an AI-powered customer security review platform).
conveyor.cloud specifically refers to Keyoti's Visual Studio tunnelling tool for IIS Express and ASP.NET developers, and this profile covers that product exclusively.

features.title: Local network device access
features.description: Exposes a running IIS Express or ASP.NET development server to other devices on the same network for cross-device testing.

features.title: Internet tunnelling
features.description: Generates a public URL that tunnels directly to the local ASP.NET application for remote access and sharing.

features.title: Visual Studio extension
features.description: Installs directly into Visual Studio, requiring no separate configuration changes to IIS Express project settings.

features.title: Standalone system tray tool
features.description: Runs outside Visual Studio in the Windows system tray and can start automatically with Windows.

features.title: Webhook testing support
features.description: The generated public URL can be used to receive webhook callbacks from services like Twilio during local development.

features.title: Custom port mappings
features.description: Pro tier supports mapping custom ports for more complex local development setups.

features.title: Custom subdomains
features.description: Pro subscribers can use custom subdomains for their tunnelled URLs instead of randomly generated ones.

features.title: No-configuration setup
features.description: Works after simple extension installation without modifying existing IIS Express or project configuration files.

pros: Very low-friction setup directly inside Visual Studio
Removes need to publish a project just to test on other devices
Useful for webhook development against local servers
Both extension and standalone tool options available
Free tier is usable for basic local network testing
cons: Limited to Windows, Visual Studio, and IIS Express/ASP.NET workflows
Not a general-purpose tunnelling tool like ngrok for other stacks
Free tier has time and feature limitations compared to Pro
Small vendor with limited public company information
Less name recognition than broader tunnelling competitors

faqs.question: What is conveyor.cloud?
faqs.answer: conveyor.cloud is the product site for Conveyor, a Visual Studio extension and standalone tool by Keyoti Inc. that tunnels a local IIS Express or ASP.NET server to other devices and the internet.

faqs.question: Is Conveyor free?
faqs.answer: Conveyor has a free tier with basic local network and limited internet tunnelling, plus a paid Pro tier priced at roughly 49 USD per developer per year for additional features.

faqs.question: What is Conveyor used for?
faqs.answer: It is primarily used for testing a local web application on other devices, sharing a local build with collaborators, and receiving webhook callbacks during development.

faqs.question: Does Conveyor require changing my project configuration?
faqs.answer: No, Conveyor is designed to work without requiring changes to IIS Express configuration or publishing the project.

faqs.question: Who makes Conveyor?
faqs.answer: Conveyor is made by Keyoti Inc., a small software company founded in 2002 and based in Comox, British Columbia, Canada.

faqs.question: Is conveyor.cloud the same as Conveyor CI or ConveyorData?
faqs.answer: No, those are unrelated products. conveyor.cloud refers specifically to Keyoti's Visual Studio tunnelling tool for IIS Express and ASP.NET.

faqs.question: What platforms does Conveyor support?
faqs.answer: Conveyor is a Windows-focused tool built for Visual Studio and IIS Express or ASP.NET development environments.

faqs.question: What does the Pro upgrade add?
faqs.answer: The Pro upgrade adds custom subdomains, removes connection time limits, and enables custom port mappings.

enrichment.short_description: Cookiefirst is a consent management platform (CMP) that helps website owners scan for tracking cookies and scripts, display a customizable consent banner, and log visitor consent to comply with GDPR, ePrivacy, CCPA, LGPD, and other privacy laws. Founded in 2019 in Amsterdam, it was acquired by iubenda (a team.blue brand) in January 2025 and integrates with Google Consent Mode v2 and major CMS and tag manager platforms.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription, billed per domain based on monthly page views and script count
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans start at 9 EUR per month (Basic)
enrichment.company_size: Small (around 9 employees as an independent company before its 2025 acquisition by iubenda/team.blue, which employs 140+ across the group)
languages: and 40+ additional languages for banner translations
enrichment.seo_title: Cookiefirst Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Compliance
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cookiefirst cookie consent platform: pricing, GDPR/CCPA compliance features, cookie scanning, Google Consent Mode v2 support, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cookiefirst is a consent management platform (CMP) founded in 2019 by brothers Kees van den Bos and Tom van den Bos in Amsterdam, Netherlands, where the company (a registered brand of Digital Data Solutions BV) remains headquartered. In January 2025, Cookiefirst was acquired by iubenda, a European privacy compliance company that operates as a brand of the Belgium-based group team.blue; iubenda itself is based in Milan, Italy, and the combined group serves more than 100,000 customers across 100-plus countries. As an independent company, Cookiefirst had around 9 employees as of mid-2024, reflecting its position as a lean, focused SaaS product prior to being folded into iubenda's broader privacy compliance suite. The core product lets website owners generate a compliant cookie consent banner, run automated and periodic scans of a site's cookies and third-party tracking scripts, and maintain a searchable, exportable log of visitor consent choices for audit purposes. Cookiefirst supports compliance with GDPR and the ePrivacy Directive in the EU, CCPA in California, LGPD in Brazil, Quebec's Law 25, and PIPEDA in Canada, and it is a Google-certified Consent Management Platform partner, meaning it integrates directly with Google Consent Mode v2 and Microsoft's Universal Event Tracking (UET) Consent Mode so that advertising and analytics tags respect user consent choices automatically. The banner and accompanying auto-generated cookie policy can be translated into more than 44 languages, and the platform supports IAB TCF v2.2 for programmatic advertising compliance. Cookiefirst integrates with Google Tag Manager and offers plugins or native integrations for WordPress, Shopify, Magento, Drupal, and at least eight other CMS and e-commerce platforms, along with a documented API for custom implementations and role-based team permissions for larger organizations. Pricing follows a freemium, per-domain model gated primarily by monthly page views, with a soft cap of 250,000 page views per domain per month (allowing a 25 percent overage buffer across two consecutive months) before custom enterprise pricing is required. Published tiers include a Free plan limited to one third-party script and a single one-time scan and language; a Basic plan around 9 EUR per month (or roughly 99 EUR annually) that unlocks unlimited scripts, monthly automated scans, and multiple languages; and a Plus plan around 19 EUR per month adding priority support with a sub-24-hour response commitment; Enterprise pricing is custom for organizations with multiple domains or very high traffic. Annual billing includes one month free compared to paying monthly. The platform reports enterprise-grade customers including Vogue, Aegon, Hyundai, Elsevier, and InterContinental Hotels, and it runs a reseller program offering 30 percent commission for agencies and consultants who resell Cookiefirst to their clients. Competing directly with CookieYes, Cookiebot, and Osano in the consent management category, Cookiefirst differentiates through its straightforward per-domain pricing, broad language support for auto-generated policies, and its post-acquisition backing by iubenda's larger privacy compliance ecosystem, which now includes complementary products like privacy policy generators and terms-of-service tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Cookiefirst Does
paragraphs: Cookiefirst is a consent management platform that scans a website for cookies and third-party tracking scripts, displays a customizable consent banner to visitors, and stores a searchable log of consent decisions for compliance audits.

It targets GDPR and ePrivacy compliance in the EU alongside CCPA, LGPD, Quebec's Law 25, and PIPEDA, and supports Google Consent Mode v2 so ad and analytics tags respect visitor consent automatically.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Cookiefirst Pricing Works
paragraphs: Pricing is per domain and scales primarily with monthly page views, with a soft limit of 250,000 page views per domain before custom pricing applies.

The Free plan covers one script and a one-time scan; Basic (around 9 EUR/month) and Plus (around 19 EUR/month) add unlimited scripts, monthly scans, more languages, and priority support; Enterprise is custom for multi-domain or high-traffic sites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Ownership and Acquisition History
paragraphs: Cookiefirst was founded in Amsterdam in 2019 by brothers Kees and Tom van den Bos and operated independently, with roughly 9 employees, until being acquired by iubenda in January 2025.

iubenda is a Milan-based privacy compliance company operating as a brand within the Belgium-headquartered team.blue group, which now positions Cookiefirst alongside its other consent management and legal-compliance products.

Smaller standalone team size compared to some larger CMP competitors

Advanced compliance frameworks like IAB TCF may require Plus or higher tiers

faqs.question: What is Cookiefirst?
faqs.answer: Cookiefirst is a consent management platform that scans websites for cookies and tracking scripts, displays a consent banner, and logs visitor consent choices for GDPR, CCPA, and other privacy law compliance.

faqs.question: How much does Cookiefirst cost?
faqs.answer: Cookiefirst offers a free plan for one script with a one-time scan, and paid plans starting around 9 EUR per month (Basic) up to around 19 EUR per month (Plus), with custom Enterprise pricing for high-traffic or multi-domain sites.

faqs.question: Who owns Cookiefirst?
faqs.answer: Cookiefirst was founded in Amsterdam in 2019 by Kees and Tom van den Bos and was acquired by iubenda, a team.blue brand, in January 2025.

faqs.question: Does Cookiefirst support Google Consent Mode v2?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cookiefirst is a Google-certified CMP with native integration for Google Consent Mode v2 and Microsoft UET Consent Mode.

faqs.question: What privacy laws does Cookiefirst help with?
faqs.answer: It supports compliance with GDPR, ePrivacy, CCPA, LGPD, Quebec's Law 25, and PIPEDA.

faqs.answer: Cookiefirst supports more than 44 languages for consent banners and auto-generated cookie policies.

faqs.question: Is there a page view limit on Cookiefirst plans?
faqs.answer: Yes, plans have a soft limit of 250,000 monthly page views per domain, with a 25 percent overage buffer, before custom pricing is needed.

faqs.question: What platforms does Cookiefirst integrate with?
faqs.answer: It integrates with WordPress, Shopify, Magento, Drupal, Google Tag Manager, and several other CMS and e-commerce platforms.

enrichment.short_description: CookieYes is a Google-certified consent management platform that automates cookie scanning, consent banners, and compliance logging for GDPR, CCPA, and other privacy laws, powering more than 1.5 million websites. It began in 2018 as the WordPress plugin Cookie Law Info and grew into a standalone CMP operated by CookieYes Limited, headquartered in Milton Keynes, England.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription, billed per domain based on pages scanned and monthly page views
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans start at 10 USD per month per domain (Basic)
enrichment.company_size: Small to mid-size (exact headcount not publicly disclosed; company registered in the UK)

enrichment.seo_title: CookieYes Review 2026: Pricing, Plans and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CookieYes cookie consent platform review: pricing tiers, GDPR/CCPA compliance features, cookie scanning, and Google Consent Mode integration.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CookieYes is a consent management platform (CMP) that automates cookie banner display, cookie scanning, and consent logging so website owners can comply with GDPR, ePrivacy, CCPA, and other global privacy regulations. The product traces back to 2018, when it launched as Cookie Law Info, a WordPress plugin; it later expanded into a standalone, platform-agnostic CMP and now operates under CookieYes Limited, a company registered in the United Kingdom (company number 13074037) and headquartered in Milton Keynes, England. CookieYes states that more than 1.5 million websites run its consent solution, spanning WordPress sites, Shopify stores, and custom-built websites, and it holds Google CMP certification, meaning it has been vetted for direct integration with Google Consent Mode V2 and the IAB Transparency and Consent Framework (TCF) v2.2/v2.3, both of which are used to control how advertising and analytics tags fire based on visitor consent. Core functionality includes an automated cookie scanner that periodically crawls a site to detect first- and third-party cookies and tracking scripts, a customizable and auto-translated consent banner, automatic blocking of non-essential cookies until consent is given, a consent log for audit and dispute purposes, and built-in legal policy generators for cookie policies and privacy notices. CookieYes uses a freemium, per-domain pricing model with four public tiers. The Free plan supports small blogs and personal sites with a 100-page scan limit and up to 5,000 monthly page views, offering basic banner customization, third-party cookie blocking, and Google Consent Mode and Do Not Track support at no cost. The Basic plan, priced around 10 USD per month per domain, raises the scan limit to 600 pages and page views to 100,000 per month (with overage billed at 0.30 USD per additional 1,000 views), and adds advanced customization such as multilingual auto-translated banners and custom CSS. The Pro plan, CookieYes's most popular tier at around 25 USD per month per domain, scans up to 4,000 pages, covers 300,000 monthly page views, and introduces geo-targeting (showing different consent flows by visitor location), monthly scheduled scanning, and full IAB TCF v2.2 and Google Consent Mode integration. The Ultimate plan, around 55 USD per month per domain, scans up to 8,000 pages, offers unlimited page views, weekly scheduled scanning, custom branding, the ability to remove CookieYes branding, scanning behind login walls, and IP whitelisting for the CookieYes scanner. All paid plans include a 14-day free trial with no charge until the trial ends, and annual billing includes roughly two months free compared to monthly billing; custom Enterprise pricing is available for large organizations with specialized needs. Because CookieYes started life as a widely adopted WordPress plugin, it retains especially deep WordPress integration alongside broader platform support, and it competes most directly with Cookiebot, Cookiefirst, and Osano in the consent management space, generally positioning itself on affordability at the entry tiers and straightforward per-domain scaling as traffic grows.

long_form_blocks.heading: What CookieYes Does
paragraphs: CookieYes is a consent management platform that scans websites for cookies and tracking scripts, displays an automated and customizable consent banner, blocks non-essential cookies until consent is granted, and keeps a compliance log of visitor consent choices.
It began in 2018 as the WordPress plugin Cookie Law Info before expanding into a standalone, platform-agnostic CMP now used on more than 1.5 million websites.

long_form_blocks.heading: CookieYes Pricing Tiers
paragraphs: CookieYes offers four public tiers: Free (up to 5,000 monthly page views), Basic (around 10 USD/month, 100,000 page views), Pro (around 25 USD/month, 300,000 page views, geo-targeting), and Ultimate (around 55 USD/month, unlimited page views, weekly scans, custom branding).
All paid tiers include a 14-day free trial, and annual billing offers roughly two months free compared to monthly pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Compliance and Integration Features
paragraphs: As a Google-certified CMP, CookieYes integrates directly with Google Consent Mode V2 and supports IAB TCF v2.2/v2.3 for programmatic advertising compliance on higher tiers.
It also includes built-in legal policy generators, geo-targeted consent flows, and scheduled automated scanning to keep the cookie inventory current as a site changes.

features.title: Automated cookie scanner
features.description: Crawls a site on a schedule to detect first- and third-party cookies and tracking scripts and keep the consent banner accurate.

features.title: Customizable, auto-translated banner
features.description: Consent banners can be styled to match brand guidelines and auto-translated for multilingual audiences.

features.title: Automatic cookie blocking
features.description: Non-essential cookies are blocked by default until a visitor grants consent.

features.title: Google Consent Mode V2 integration

faqs.question: What is CookieYes?
faqs.answer: CookieYes is a consent management platform that automates cookie scanning, consent banners, and compliance logging for GDPR, CCPA, and other privacy laws.

faqs.question: How much does CookieYes cost?
faqs.answer: CookieYes has a free plan for small sites and paid plans per domain starting around 10 USD/month (Basic), 25 USD/month (Pro), and 55 USD/month (Ultimate).

faqs.question: When was CookieYes founded?
faqs.answer: CookieYes began in 2018 as the WordPress plugin Cookie Law Info before growing into a standalone consent management platform.

faqs.question: Where is CookieYes headquartered?
faqs.answer: CookieYes is operated by CookieYes Limited, registered in the United Kingdom and headquartered in Milton Keynes, England.

faqs.question: Does CookieYes support Google Consent Mode?
faqs.answer: Yes, CookieYes is a Google-certified CMP with native integration for Google Consent Mode V2.

faqs.question: Is CookieYes free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free plan is available for small blogs and personal sites with up to 5,000 monthly page views, though it has limited features compared to paid tiers.

faqs.question: How many websites use CookieYes?
faqs.answer: CookieYes states that more than 1.5 million websites run its consent management solution.

faqs.question: What is the difference between CookieYes Pro and Ultimate?
faqs.answer: Pro adds geo-targeting and monthly scheduled scanning with 300,000 monthly page views, while Ultimate offers unlimited page views, weekly scans, custom branding, and the ability to remove CookieYes branding.

enrichment.short_description: This profile covers Copilot Money (copilot.money), a subscription personal finance app for tracking spending, budgets, investments, and net worth, founded in 2020 and headquartered in New York. It is distinguished here from Copilot.com (the agency client-portal platform, which rebranded to Assembly in 2025), GitHub Copilot, and Microsoft Copilot, none of which currently operate under the standalone Copilot brand name.
enrichment.pricing_model: Subscription (no free tier beyond a limited trial)
enrichment.starting_price: 13 USD per month, or 95 USD billed annually
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Copilot Money Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Copilot Money app review: pricing, budgeting and net worth tracking features, iOS/Mac/web support, and how it compares to other finance apps.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: This entry covers Copilot Money, a personal finance and budgeting app available at copilot.money, and it is important to disambiguate this from several other products that also use the name Copilot. Copilot Money was founded in 2020 and is headquartered in New York City, with roughly 33 employees as of recent counts; it raised a Series A funding round of 6 million USD to build out its product. It was designed natively for Apple platforms from the start, launching first on iPhone, iPad, and Mac, and it added a limited web app in December 2025 that, as of early 2026, supports transaction filtering and basic metrics but is not a full replacement for the native mobile and desktop apps. The app connects to tens of thousands of financial institutions through account aggregation, with direct native integrations called out for services like Venmo, Apple Card, and Coinbase, and it uses AI-assisted categorization to automatically sort transactions, track spending trends over time, monitor investment account performance, and calculate net worth in one consolidated view. It operates on a pure subscription model with no persistent free tier: pricing is 13 USD per month or 95 USD per year (about 7.92 USD/month equivalent) on annual billing, with a one-month free trial available through the App Store and occasional extended trials via user referral codes. This entry deliberately does not cover three other prominent products that share the 'Copilot' name: (1) Copilot.com, a client portal, billing, e-signature, and CRM platform for service agencies, which rebranded to 'Assembly' in 2025 and therefore no longer operates under the standalone Copilot brand as of 2026; (2) GitHub Copilot, Microsoft/GitHub's AI pair-programming assistant integrated into code editors; and (3) Microsoft Copilot, the AI assistant embedded across Microsoft 365 and Windows. Because gappsy.com catalogs standalone named tools, and because Copilot.com's operating brand changed to Assembly, Copilot Money is the product that most closely and currently matches a standalone 'Copilot' listing distinct from the larger platform-embedded Copilot products from Microsoft and GitHub. Copilot Money's category peers include other premium budgeting and net-worth apps such as Monarch Money, YNAB (You Need A Budget), and Rocket Money, and it is frequently reviewed favorably by finance publications for its design quality and Apple-native experience, though its historical lack of an Android app and reliance on a paid-only model (versus some competitors' free tiers) are commonly cited drawbacks in third-party reviews. Prospective users evaluating Copilot Money should weigh its premium-only pricing and until-recently iOS/Mac-only availability against its polished interface, automated categorization accuracy, and investment-tracking depth compared to lower-cost or free budgeting alternatives.

long_form_blocks.heading: Which 'Copilot' This Profile Covers
paragraphs: The name Copilot is shared by multiple unrelated products: Copilot Money (a personal finance app), Copilot.com (an agency client-portal platform that rebranded to Assembly in 2025), GitHub Copilot (an AI coding assistant), and Microsoft Copilot (Microsoft's AI assistant across its productivity suite).

This profile covers Copilot Money at copilot.money because it is the product still actively operating under the standalone 'Copilot' brand name as of 2026; Copilot.com no longer uses that name after its Assembly rebrand, and GitHub Copilot and Microsoft Copilot are sub-brands of much larger platforms rather than standalone products.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Copilot Money Does
paragraphs: Copilot Money is a subscription budgeting and net-worth tracking app that connects to bank, credit card, and investment accounts to automatically categorize transactions using AI, track spending trends, and monitor investment performance.
Built natively for Apple platforms first, it launched on iPhone, iPad, and Mac before adding a limited web app in December 2025.

long_form_blocks.heading: Copilot Money Pricing
paragraphs: Copilot Money charges 13 USD per month or 95 USD billed annually, with no persistent free tier beyond a one-month App Store trial.
Users can sometimes extend their trial to two or three months using referral codes shared by existing subscribers.

faqs.answer: Copilot Money is a subscription personal finance app that tracks spending, budgets, investments, and net worth, built natively for Apple devices with a limited web app added in December 2025.

faqs.question: How much does Copilot Money cost?
faqs.answer: Copilot Money costs 13 USD per month or 95 USD billed annually, with a one-month free trial available through the App Store.

faqs.question: Is Copilot Money the same as GitHub Copilot or Microsoft Copilot?
faqs.answer: No, Copilot Money is an unrelated personal finance app. GitHub Copilot is an AI coding assistant and Microsoft Copilot is Microsoft's AI assistant across its productivity products.

faqs.question: Is Copilot Money the same as Copilot.com?
faqs.answer: No, Copilot.com was a client portal and billing platform for service agencies that rebranded to Assembly in 2025 and no longer operates under the Copilot name.

faqs.question: Does Copilot Money have an Android app?
faqs.answer: As of 2026, Copilot Money remains focused on iPhone, iPad, and Mac, with a limited web app added in December 2025; there is no dedicated Android app.

faqs.answer: Copilot Money was founded in 2020 and is headquartered in New York City.

enrichment.short_description: Fedora Copr, hosted at copr.fedorainfracloud.org, is a free, community build system that lets anyone create and host custom RPM package repositories built from source RPMs, without needing them accepted into the official Fedora repositories. Sponsored by Red Hat and run within Fedora Project infrastructure, it uses the Mock chroot tool to build packages and is commonly used for third-party repositories, continuous integration, and mass rebuild testing.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free (open source, community/sponsored infrastructure, no paid tiers)
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable (community project sponsored by Red Hat within the Fedora Project; no independent company or employee count)
enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable (hosted on Fedora Project infrastructure; Red Hat, the primary sponsor, is headquartered in Raleigh, North Carolina, United States)
enrichment.seo_title: Fedora Copr (copr.fedorainfracloud.org): Free RPM Build System
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What is Fedora Copr? A free, Red Hat-sponsored build system for hosting custom RPM package repositories, with mock-based chroot builds and API access.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Fedora Copr, accessible at copr.fedorainfracloud.org, is a free, lightweight build system that allows individual contributors and organizations to build software packages from source RPM files and automatically publish them as installable YUM/DNF repositories, without those packages needing to go through the review and inclusion process required for the official Fedora package repositories. It exists specifically to solve a common Linux packaging problem: a developer or project maintainer wants to distribute RPM packages for Fedora, RHEL, CentOS Stream, or other RPM-based distributions, but their software either does not yet qualify for or has not yet been submitted to the official distribution repositories. Copr fills that gap by providing free, self-service infrastructure where any user with a Fedora Account System (FAS) account can create a project, upload or point to a source RPM (SRPM), and have Copr build binary packages across multiple target chroots (combinations of distribution version and CPU architecture) automatically. Internally, Copr builds packages using Mock, the same chroot-based build environment manager that powers official Fedora and Koji builds, ensuring build isolation and reproducibility consistent with Fedora's own packaging standards; resulting repositories are cryptographically signed. Copr supports both standard chroots matching released Fedora, EPEL, and other distribution versions, and 'custom chroots,' which give project owners more manual control over the build environment for edge cases not covered by standard targets. The service is designed to scale to very large workloads: it can sustain mass rebuilds involving thousands of packages, a capability used within the Fedora community for tasks like rebuilding the entire package set against a proposed toolchain change or rebuilding language-ecosystem packages (such as Python PyPI packages or Ruby gems) as RPMs at scale. Common use cases beyond official Fedora packaging work include hosting third-party or 'copr' repositories that end users add manually to install software not available upstream, running continuous integration builds that need a real RPM output rather than just a compiled binary, and providing beta or nightly builds of a project ahead of formal release. Copr is sponsored by Red Hat and operates on servers deployed within Fedora Project infrastructure, working closely with the Fedora Infrastructure team, though Copr documentation notes it is not officially guaranteed-supported Fedora infrastructure in the same way core Fedora services are, meaning it should not be relied upon for absolutely mission-critical production package hosting without independent verification. Because it is a community and vendor-sponsored open source project rather than a company, Copr has no founding year, headquarters, pricing plans, or employee count in the traditional commercial sense; it is entirely free to use, and its source code is maintained publicly on GitHub under the fedora-copr organization, with a Python client library (python-copr) available for programmatic and automated interaction with the build service. For developers and system administrators, Copr functions similarly in spirit to other community package-hosting or CI-adjacent build services, but is uniquely tied to the RPM and Fedora/RHEL ecosystem rather than being distribution-agnostic.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Fedora Copr Does
paragraphs: Fedora Copr is a free build system that turns a source RPM into a signed, installable package repository, letting anyone host third-party RPM repositories without going through official Fedora package review.
Users with a Fedora Account System (FAS) account create a project, submit a source RPM, and Copr builds binaries across selected distribution and architecture combinations automatically.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Builds Work
paragraphs: Copr uses Mock, the same chroot-based build environment tool used for official Fedora and Koji builds, to isolate and build each package, and it signs the resulting repositories.

It supports both standard chroots matching released Fedora and EPEL versions and custom chroots for cases needing manual environment configuration, and it can sustain mass rebuilds of thousands of packages at once.

long_form_blocks.heading: Governance, Hosting, and Access
paragraphs: Copr is sponsored by Red Hat and runs on infrastructure deployed within the Fedora Project, working closely with the Fedora Infrastructure team, though it is not officially guaranteed-supported infrastructure in the same way as core Fedora services.
The project is entirely free, open source, and maintained publicly on GitHub under the fedora-copr organization, with a Python client library available for programmatic access.

features.title: Mock-based build isolation
features.description: Uses Mock, the same chroot build tool behind official Fedora and Koji builds, for consistent, isolated build environments.

features.title: Custom chroots
features.description: Allows manual configuration of build environments for packages that do not fit standard distribution targets.

features.title: Mass rebuild capability
features.description: Can sustain rebuilding thousands of packages at once, used for Fedora-wide toolchain or ecosystem rebuild testing.

features.title: Signed repository output
features.description: Resulting package repositories are cryptographically signed for integrity and trust.

features.title: Free self-service project creation
features.description: Any user with a Fedora Account System account can create and manage build projects without cost.

features.title: API and Python client library
features.description: Provides a documented API and the python-copr client for programmatic and automated build management.

pros: Completely free to use with no paid tiers
Uses the same trusted Mock build tooling as official Fedora infrastructure
Scales to mass rebuilds of thousands of packages
Signed repository output for package integrity
Open source project code publicly available on GitHub
Low barrier to entry with just a Fedora Account System account
cons: Not officially guaranteed-supported Fedora infrastructure
Limited to RPM-based distributions, not usable for other package formats
Documentation and workflows assume familiarity with RPM packaging and Mock
No commercial support tier or SLA for critical production use
Interface and tooling are aimed at technical Linux packagers, not general users

faqs.question: What is Fedora Copr?
faqs.answer: Fedora Copr is a free build system at copr.fedorainfracloud.org that builds source RPMs into signed, installable package repositories for Fedora and other RPM-based distributions.

faqs.question: Is Fedora Copr free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Copr is entirely free and open source with no paid pricing tiers.

faqs.question: Who runs Fedora Copr?
faqs.answer: Copr is sponsored by Red Hat and hosted on Fedora Project infrastructure, maintained by the Fedora Copr community and the Fedora Infrastructure team.

faqs.question: Do I need an account to use Copr?
faqs.answer: Yes, you need a Fedora Account System (FAS) account to create and manage build projects on Copr.

faqs.question: What build tool does Copr use internally?
faqs.answer: Copr uses Mock, the same chroot-based build environment manager used for official Fedora and Koji package builds.

faqs.question: Can Copr handle large-scale rebuilds?
faqs.answer: Yes, Copr can sustain mass rebuilds involving thousands of packages, such as Fedora-wide toolchain change testing or rebuilding language-ecosystem packages as RPMs.

faqs.question: Is Copr officially supported Fedora infrastructure?
faqs.answer: Copr documentation notes it is not yet officially guaranteed-supported Fedora infrastructure, though it runs within Fedora Project infrastructure and works closely with the Fedora Infrastructure team.

faqs.question: What is Copr commonly used for?
faqs.answer: Common uses include hosting third-party RPM repositories, continuous integration builds, nightly or beta package builds, and mass rebuild testing.

use_cases.title: Hosting third-party RPM repositories

use_cases.title: Mass ecosystem rebuild testing
use_cases.description: Fedora contributors use Copr to rebuild large sets of packages, such as language-ecosystem modules, to test toolchain or dependency changes at scale.
use_cases.audience: Fedora Project contributors and distribution maintainers

enrichment.short_description: Copyleaks is an AI-powered content integrity platform offering plagiarism detection, AI-generated text detection, source code plagiarism detection (Codeleaks), and AI image detection. Founded in 2015 by Alon Yamin and Yehonatan Bitton and headquartered in New York with an Israel-based team, it serves education, publishing, and enterprise customers through a web app, browser extension, API, and LMS integrations with Canvas, Moodle, and Blackboard.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium credit-based subscription, plus separate enterprise and API/model-integration pricing
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available (~10 pages/month); paid individual plans start at 7.99 USD per month
enrichment.company_size: Small to mid-size (reported at roughly 20 employees in 2024, growing to approximately 92 employees across three continents by early 2026)
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States (with a significant Tel Aviv, Israel operations base)
languages: and AI/plagiarism detection support across 30+ languages
enrichment.seo_title: Copyleaks Review 2026: AI Detector and Plagiarism Checker Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Copyleaks review: AI content detection and plagiarism checker pricing, accuracy, LMS integrations, and enterprise API options for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Copyleaks is a content authenticity and integrity platform that combines plagiarism detection with AI-generated content detection in a single product, founded in 2015 by software developers Alon Yamin and Yehonatan Bitton, who specialized in text analysis and machine learning. The company is headquartered in New York, New York, while maintaining a significant operational base in Tel Aviv, Israel, reflecting its origins as an Israeli startup; reported headcount has grown from around 20 employees in 2024 to approximately 92 employees across three continents by early 2026, and the company raised 7.75 million USD in funding in 2022 to expand its anti-plagiarism and AI detection capabilities. The core product scans submitted text and simultaneously checks it against a plagiarism database spanning the open web, academic publications, and internal/institutional repositories, while also running an AI content detector that claims over 99 percent accuracy and coverage of outputs from major language models including GPT, Gemini, and Claude, updated as new models are released; both plagiarism and AI-detection results appear together in a single combined report. Beyond text, Copyleaks offers Codeleaks, a specialized detector for identifying AI-generated or plagiarized source code, and an AI Image Detector that uses forensic image analysis and machine learning to flag AI-generated or manipulated images, presenting a visual overlay highlighting the specific regions most likely to be altered or synthetic. The platform integrates directly into learning management systems including Canvas, Moodle, and Blackboard, positioning it heavily toward educational institutions checking student submissions, alongside a Chrome browser extension, a web application, and a full API for developers who want to embed detection into their own products; it is also distributed via the Google Workspace Marketplace as an AI and Plagiarism Checker add-on. Pricing follows a freemium, credit-based subscription model. The free plan offers roughly 10 pages (about 2,500 words) of scanning per month, limited to two concurrent scans with lower processing priority, suitable only for occasional spot checks. Paid individual plans are priced separately or bundled: an AI Content Detector-only plan costs 7.99 USD per month for 1,200 credits (up to roughly 300,000 words) with two user seats and API access; a Plagiarism Detector-only plan costs 8.99 USD per month for the same credit allotment; and a combined AI and Plagiarism Detection plan costs 13.99 USD per month for both capabilities sharing the same credit pool. An annual option is also available, billed at 899.88 USD per year, which includes 25 user seats, making it more suitable for small teams or departments than the monthly individual plans. For large organizations, enterprise-grade model integration pricing starts at 4,500 USD per year, reflecting deeper API access and higher volume needs. Independent academic studies cited in third-party reviews show mixed accuracy results for AI detection generally across the industry, with Copyleaks specifically demonstrating around 93 percent sensitivity in detecting GPT-4-generated content in one study, while struggling more with text that has been manually edited or paraphrased after AI generation, a known limitation shared by most AI detectors. Copyleaks competes most directly with Turnitin, GPTZero, and Originality.ai in the combined plagiarism and AI-detection category, differentiating through its bundled multi-modal detection (text, code, and images) in one platform and its deep LMS integrations for academic institutions.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Copyleaks Does
paragraphs: Copyleaks combines plagiarism detection and AI-generated content detection into a single scan and report, checking submitted text against web, academic, and internal content sources while also flagging AI-generated writing from models like GPT, Gemini, and Claude.

Beyond text, it offers Codeleaks for detecting AI-generated or plagiarized source code, and an AI Image Detector that uses forensic analysis to flag AI-generated or manipulated images with a visual overlay of suspect regions.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Copyleaks Pricing Works
paragraphs: A free plan offers around 10 pages of scanning per month for occasional spot checks. Individual paid plans range from 7.99 USD/month (AI detection only) to 13.99 USD/month (combined AI and plagiarism detection), each including 1,200 credits covering up to roughly 300,000 words.

An annual plan at 899.88 USD per year includes 25 user seats for small teams, while enterprise model-integration pricing starts at 4,500 USD per year for larger organizations needing deeper API access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Accuracy and Known Limitations
paragraphs: Copyleaks reports over 99 percent accuracy for AI detection across major language model outputs, and independent studies have found around 93 percent sensitivity for detecting GPT-4-generated content specifically.

Like other AI detectors industry-wide, accuracy drops when AI-generated text has been manually edited or paraphrased afterward, a limitation worth factoring into high-stakes decisions such as academic integrity cases.

features.description: Paid plans include multiple user seats and a shared credit pool, suiting small teams as well as individuals.

Company headcount is still relatively small, at under 100 employees as of early 2026

faqs.question: What is Copyleaks?
faqs.answer: Copyleaks is an AI-powered platform that detects plagiarism, AI-generated text, AI-generated source code, and AI-generated or manipulated images, offering combined reports for text integrity checks.

faqs.question: How much does Copyleaks cost?
faqs.answer: Individual plans range from 7.99 USD/month for AI detection only up to 13.99 USD/month for combined AI and plagiarism detection, with a free plan offering about 10 pages per month and enterprise pricing starting at 4,500 USD per year.

faqs.question: How accurate is Copyleaks' AI detector?
faqs.answer: Copyleaks claims over 99 percent accuracy across major language models, though independent studies show around 93 percent sensitivity for GPT-4-generated content specifically, with lower accuracy on edited or paraphrased AI text.

faqs.question: Does Copyleaks integrate with learning management systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, Copyleaks integrates directly with Canvas, Moodle, and Blackboard for academic institutions checking student submissions.

faqs.question: When was Copyleaks founded?
faqs.answer: Copyleaks was founded in 2015 by Alon Yamin and Yehonatan Bitton.

faqs.question: Where is Copyleaks based?
faqs.answer: Copyleaks is headquartered in New York, New York, with a significant operations base in Tel Aviv, Israel.

faqs.question: Can Copyleaks detect plagiarized source code?
faqs.answer: Yes, through Codeleaks, a specialized tool for identifying AI-generated or plagiarized source code.

faqs.question: Does Copyleaks have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the free plan offers around 10 pages (about 2,500 words) of scanning per month with limited concurrent scans.

enrichment.short_description: CopyQ is a free, open-source clipboard manager for Windows, macOS, and Linux that saves clipboard history and lets users search, edit, tag, and organize copied text, images, and files across tabs. Maintained by developer Lukas Holecek since 2009 under the GPLv3 license, it includes a scripting and command-line interface and can sync clipboard items across machines via a shared folder.

enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable (open-source project; maintainer based in the Czech Republic)
enrichment.seo_title: CopyQ Review 2026: Free Open-Source Clipboard Manager
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CopyQ clipboard manager review: features, scripting, cross-device sync, and how it compares to other free clipboard tools for Windows, macOS, and Linux.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CopyQ is a free, open-source clipboard manager originally created and still actively maintained by developer Lukas Holecek, with development history dating back to 2009 and the project accumulating over 9,000 stars on its GitHub repository. It runs on Windows 7 through 11, macOS 10.15 and later, and most major Linux distributions, presenting the same interface and feature set across all three platforms so users do not need to relearn the tool when switching operating systems. At its core, CopyQ monitors the system clipboard and automatically saves a history of everything copied, including plain text, rich text, images, and files, storing that history in a searchable, editable list organized into user-defined tabs. Users can attach notes to individual clipboard entries for extra context, apply tags to group and filter related items, edit clipboard content directly before pasting it again, and reorder items within the history. Beyond basic clipboard history, CopyQ includes a powerful command-line interface and a built-in scripting engine that let advanced users automate clipboard-related tasks, create custom commands triggered by clipboard content matching specific patterns, and build custom workflows, which is a key differentiator from simpler clipboard history tools that only offer a fixed list with no automation layer. CopyQ also supports syncing clipboard items across multiple machines by pointing the application at a shared folder, such as one managed by Dropbox, Google Drive, or a private network drive, allowing multi-device clipboard continuity without requiring a proprietary cloud service, a subscription, or a user account of any kind. The software is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3), which guarantees the source code will remain free, viewable, modifiable, and redistributable in perpetuity; there is no trial period, freemium tier, or paid upgrade path, as the entire feature set is available to every user at no cost. CopyQ is under active development, with recent major releases including version 13.0.0 in October 2025 and version 16.0.0 in May 2026, reflecting a steady release cadence for bug fixes, platform compatibility updates, and incremental feature additions. As an independent open-source project rather than a company, CopyQ has no headquarters, employee count, or founding company in the commercial sense; it is maintained primarily by Lukas Holecek along with community contributors submitting patches and translations through GitHub. In the clipboard manager category, CopyQ is most often compared to Ditto (Windows-only, also free and open source), Maccy and Paste (macOS-focused, with Paste being paid), and various built-in OS clipboard history tools; CopyQ differentiates itself through genuine cross-platform parity, its scripting and automation capabilities, and its no-cost, no-account model, making it a favorite among developers, power users, and anyone who copies and pastes frequently across multiple devices without wanting to rely on a cloud-synced commercial product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What CopyQ Does
paragraphs: CopyQ is a clipboard manager that automatically saves everything copied to the system clipboard, including text, images, and files, into a searchable, editable history organized by tabs.
Users can attach notes, apply tags, reorder items, and edit clipboard entries before pasting them again, going well beyond the single-item memory of a default OS clipboard.

long_form_blocks.heading: Scripting, Automation, and Sync
paragraphs: CopyQ includes a command-line interface and a built-in scripting engine, letting advanced users automate clipboard workflows and trigger custom commands based on clipboard content.
Clipboard items can be synced across multiple machines by pointing CopyQ at a shared folder such as Dropbox or Google Drive, avoiding the need for a proprietary cloud clipboard service or account.

long_form_blocks.heading: Licensing and Ongoing Development
paragraphs: CopyQ is released under the GNU General Public License v3.0 and has been maintained by developer Lukas Holecek since 2009, with all features available free of charge and no paid tiers.
The project remains actively developed, with major releases including version 13.0.0 in October 2025 and version 16.0.0 in May 2026.

features.title: Automatic clipboard history
features.description: Continuously saves everything copied to the clipboard, including text, images, and files, into a searchable history.

features.title: Tabs and organization
features.description: Clipboard items can be organized into user-defined tabs for easier navigation of large histories.

features.title: Notes and tags
features.description: Users can attach notes to clipboard entries and apply tags to group and filter related items.

features.title: In-place editing
features.description: Clipboard content can be edited directly within CopyQ before being pasted again.

features.title: Command-line interface
features.description: A full CLI allows scripting and automation of clipboard operations from the terminal.

features.title: Built-in scripting engine
features.description: Advanced users can write custom scripts and commands triggered by clipboard content patterns.

features.title: Cross-device sync via shared folder
features.description: Clipboard history can sync across machines using a shared folder like Dropbox or Google Drive, without a proprietary cloud account.

features.title: Cross-platform parity
features.description: Identical interface and feature set on Windows, macOS, and Linux, so no relearning is needed across operating systems.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers
Consistent feature set and interface across Windows, macOS, and Linux
Powerful scripting and command-line automation not found in simpler clipboard tools
No account or cloud service required for cross-device sync
Actively maintained with regular releases
Supports images and files, not just plain text
cons: Interface can feel dated or utilitarian compared to some paid competitors
Scripting and automation features have a learning curve for non-technical users
Cross-device sync depends on manually configuring a shared folder rather than built-in cloud sync
Primarily maintained by a small team, so support relies on community and GitHub issues
No official mobile app for iOS or Android

faqs.question: What is CopyQ?
faqs.answer: CopyQ is a free, open-source clipboard manager for Windows, macOS, and Linux that saves clipboard history and lets users search, edit, tag, and organize copied text, images, and files.

faqs.question: Is CopyQ free?
faqs.answer: Yes, CopyQ is entirely free and open source under the GPLv3 license, with no paid tiers, trial periods, or premium upgrades.

faqs.question: Who develops CopyQ?
faqs.answer: CopyQ has been maintained by developer Lukas Holecek since 2009, with contributions from the open-source community.

faqs.question: Can CopyQ sync clipboard history across devices?
faqs.answer: Yes, CopyQ can sync clipboard items across machines by pointing the app at a shared folder such as Dropbox or Google Drive, without requiring a cloud account.

faqs.question: Does CopyQ support scripting?
faqs.answer: Yes, CopyQ includes a command-line interface and a built-in scripting engine for automating clipboard-related tasks.

faqs.question: What platforms does CopyQ support?
faqs.answer: CopyQ runs on Windows 7 through 11, macOS 10.15 and later, and most major Linux distributions with the same feature set on each.

faqs.question: Does CopyQ store images and files, or only text?
faqs.answer: CopyQ stores text, rich text, images, and files in its clipboard history, not just plain text.

faqs.question: How often is CopyQ updated?
faqs.answer: CopyQ is actively developed, with recent major releases including version 13.0.0 in October 2025 and version 16.0.0 in May 2026.

use_cases.title: Developer productivity and scripting
use_cases.description: Developers use CopyQ's scripting and command-line interface to automate repetitive clipboard-related tasks.

use_cases.title: Cross-platform clipboard consistency
use_cases.description: Users who switch between Windows, macOS, and Linux machines rely on CopyQ for an identical clipboard manager experience on each.

use_cases.title: Multi-device clipboard sync without a cloud account
use_cases.description: Users sync clipboard history between a desktop and laptop via a shared Dropbox or Google Drive folder, avoiding proprietary cloud clipboard services.
